speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 10, 1997. We are interviewing Dr. William Perl. This is tape number seven. I'd like to thank you for letting us come back into your home, so we can finish our interview. You were telling me about the events of September 1, 1939, when the war broke out. And you had just told me, a moment ago, how vivid it is in your mind. Can you tell me what stands out in your mind about that date?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I expected the war to start any hour, not any day. Because the few days before, the pact between Ribbentrop and Stalin had been announced. So it was clear that Hitler had now free hands in the East, so that he can attack in the West.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I figured-- I asked myself, what will happen here? What will happen to our transport? The first thing that was clear to me was that Europe will be cut in two parts, along the axis-- Germany and Italy. And I was on the wrong side, here, because I could smuggle people from there, but I couldn't get them into Greece if it was under German control. Because the Germans had, among themselves, quite some debate about whether they should favor this illegal transportation or not.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, a few days before-- maybe two days before the 1st of September-- I went. It was my idea. I thought, I have to get to the train station. Everybody will want to get to the train station, from the hotel. So I went to the rescue squad and spoke, there, to the manager or officer, whoever it was, and told him that I wanted-- I make a substantial contribution for his rescue squad, but I will need him, when the war starts, [INAUDIBLE], he should be ready to wait for my call, and then he has to come and pick me up. And that's what they did. When I heard-- in the morning, when I woke up, I heard that Germany had invaded Poland. And I went to the phone first, and called the rescue squad that they should call for me. Now, I went down to pay, but there was a long line. Everybody wanted to leave. Everybody wanted to get home. The war had started. It was a big hotel. It was one of the best hotels in Europe, the Athenee Palace in-- no, it was called the Grande Bretagne Hotel, in Athens.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stood in line there, but it was very, very slow. It was agonizingly slow, where they paid their bills. You had, first, to go to the desk. And there, they gave you the bill [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Then you went to another place. It was just across the hallway, where you had to pay. So I left, and I thought it moves very slowly, and I'll be back. Will be three or four points later, but I'll have made arrangements already. I'll be able to leave, right after this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the desk. I got my bill. And when I came back, there was a long line. Many people had come in the meanwhile. I was among the first ones in, who had tried to flee.

speaker: interviewee
text: But many people had been-- five minutes, or three minutes, or eight minutes later than I-- earlier than I. So I had to wait there. And in the meanwhile, I knew that this guy will be out there. Because, I called him, and I was afraid that he might lose-- he might leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went, left the line. I told the man in front of me, and behind me, I have to relieve myself. And so they said, OK. We'll keep you this place. So I went outside and told this man, who was standing out there with [INAUDIBLE] people, [INAUDIBLE] the first war casualty, that the ambulance is here. And I went, [INAUDIBLE] and was sitting next to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this trip was one of the most lively experiences I ever had. He raced through the city. It's not very near to the railroad station. He raced through the city. He was making whistle and horn and all kinds of noises. And the streets were full of people. Everybody was out, to discuss things, where are discuss in the streets, there, with their friends and their neighbors that the war had broken out. And there were people with donkeys, and others drove their sheep across the street. And so all this noise, we came with all the noise that sheep make. And finally, we got to the railroad station. And we came there about 20 minutes or 30 minutes before the train-- which was the Orient Express-- was to leave. The Orient Express went from Europe, from central Europe, into-- split into two parts. One went to the north, to Istanbul. And the other one went to the south, to Athens. And this was the southern link of it. And I was there in time for the-- about half an hour before the train left. I was there. I got a seat. And everybody came running. But we stayed for another hour or two, before we left. And we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also have a train reservation in advance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had train reservation, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was part of your preparation.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was part of my preparation. I had made a train reservation for every day, for every day for the next week, in advance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go, when you left Athens?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Zagreb, because I wanted to get to Switzerland. And none of those countries borders Switzerland. And when I come from Zagreb, I could go either via Italy or-- it was the only way to get, via Italy. And in Zagreb, I took a taxi. He was amazed at the distance. I took a taxi to Milan. And so we crossed on the border. There was just one soldier there. He didn't take the main road. He went on a side road. And he looked at his list, that I am on the list. I was not on the list, so he let us pass. But I was afraid that the conditions are changed, and they might not let anybody in, in spite of the visas. And I had visas. I told you, I think, how I got the visas-- through the British visa which I had. And I went to Milan. And in Milan was a group, a revisionist group. And the man in charge was a Major [? Kareski. ?] And I knew him superficially, but he knew me, also superficially. But we knew each other well. And I came to him and told him, I have to get into Switzerland, because only from there can I go on organizing things. Now it was a thing which I don't think I wrote about it, in I had a passport, which I had really gotten in Bucharest. And on this passport, this German official wanted to be very nice, and he didn't stamp the J into it. But it said, [INAUDIBLE] shows that I was a Jew. But the red J was not in it. So he says, I said, I need the J. And he says, I'll have one done by a printer. And he has put a J into-- I has got the J, red J into my name, because I was afraid if I come to Switzerland, if I'm a German, I'm-- a citizen of a warring party, and they might not let me in. It was better to be a Jew, a refugee, than to be a German. That's what I figured. And I went to a small town on the lake-- I don't know which one of the lakes it is. It was one of the Swiss, Italian lakes, it is. And I forgot the name of the town. And I had to wait there. It was still early in the morning. I left at about 2:00 in the morning. We were there at about 5:00. It was dark and raining. And then a very sleepy, grouchy official came-- a customs official-- and went on this boat. It was just a motor boat. And he went from one to the other, checked the passports. And I gave him my passport. Every time you-- I was already in America for many years, and every time I crossed the border and tried to show the American passport, I was somewhat fidgety. He took my passport. He saw [INAUDIBLE] visa, and I was in. And we arrived in Lugano. And in Lugano, I did some sightseeing. I had not been in Lugano before. I was very much impressed by two of the art objects. One was the dying Socrates in a park, and the other one was a statue of desolation, [INAUDIBLE], a woman, completely blank, the face-- very beautiful, very powerful. And then I continued towards Zurich. And in Zurich, in the meanwhile, I had spoken from Italy. I had spoken to-- I don't know to whom, anymore. But I knew that the representative of the [INAUDIBLE] at this and this address. This was Reuben Hecht. And I went to this address, which was the [INAUDIBLE] of Mr. [INAUDIBLE] in the Schuetzengasse, S-C-H-U-E-T-Z-E-N-G-A-S-S-E. A little [INAUDIBLE] and there, I met Reuben Hecht. I had met him before, but only as a member of the congress, of the revisionist congress. And then of the founding congress of the new Zionist organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew each other by name, and we had met. But I don't think we'd exchanged 10 words. And he said, I'm here for the [? Yagoona ?] and the representative of the [INAUDIBLE]. And I'm very happy that we meet. I said, I am very happy about it. We have something else. He says, what? I don't remember the details.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we decided that it is not economic to take boats, which take 600 to 800 people. And we decided to take a big ship, one which takes 2,000 people. We had never before taken a ship with 2,000 people. And I got, from there, in touch with Romania, with a Mr.-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I forgot the name, now.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was an important man, in the whole affair. Maybe it'll come to my mind. He was the owner of a shipping agency-- Rand, Mr. Rand, R-A-N-D. And told him we need a ship for 2,000 people. And, when? I said, tomorrow. This was probably in October.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meanwhile, I was contacted by French intelligence, came to me with a man, [INAUDIBLE] who knew what I was doing, and said you have the possibility to get around everywhere, because you are getting the Jews out. And the Germans like that. And you know, [INAUDIBLE] what is a issue now, the survival or not survival of Europe. Work for us. And I told him, right away, I cannot, because I risk the whole transport. If I am arrested, then everyone who works for us in the countries controlled by Germany is arrested and killed. I said, no [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in touch, now, with Rand. And Rand said that he-- should I go in so much detail? OK, and Rand said, after two or three days, that he has a ship. She's [INAUDIBLE] Turkish citizen. But he has a ship that's name is It's a Turkish ship, which would be just right for 2,000 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, he told me, these men will not come to the port to pick you up. According to the general procedure which we had-- the [INAUDIBLE] was out, now. He was scared. The war had started. [INAUDIBLE]. He said, keep me out, for the time being. If I see my pictures [INAUDIBLE], I'll be available here. And this Rand bought a neutral ship, Turkey, a Turkish ship. And he said, this man-- yeah, with the [INAUDIBLE], with the arrangement that we paid, when the people went on board, not before. I was standing on the entrance, on the plank, there. And when five people were in, I gave him the 60 pounds-- 12 pounds a person. And another five, another 50 pounds, and so on. But this Turk, who did not know us, if they could rely on us, said he wants 2,000 pounds in advance. It was impossible for us to get 2,000 pounds. And meanwhile, our group in Czechoslovakia-- which was founded by my cousin Robert Mendler, M-E-N-D-L-E-R. I put him in charge for-- there was nothing going on in Czechoslovakia. And I put him in charge to create that-- he was a real go-getter. A real go-getter. Not a Zionist, but he was after money, and very capable, very bright-- bright expression. He knew life. And he said, yeah, there's no problem for me. I'll leave Saturday morning, [INAUDIBLE] to the authorities in [INAUDIBLE] of the Betar. And the Betar organized his trip, too, independently. There was two organizations, which were actually one. They were not actually one, because I was not under the command of the center in London, or in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did it-- I had some trouble with them, because of this. Because they wanted me to go, and to discuss everything with them. And I said, no, [? maybe ?] I cannot do it, to give account what's happened to the money. I can't write down that-- it's worth nothing to you, anyhow, because how do you know that I gave this guy 50 pounds, or 5 pounds as a bribe? And I don't have the time to write down, in the evening, what I spend. So we were two organizations, but still working very closely together. And they had been pressured by Eichmann to leave. And Eichmann had told them, he said, don't leave. Within a very short time, three days or so, and they all go to a concentration camp. And I called Mendler and told him, I would have a ship. But I don't have the money. So I'm sorry. I can't do anything for you. He says, how much do you need? I said, 2,000 pounds. He says, I have them. I was ready to sit down. You have 2,000 pounds, yes? How do you get that? I didn't ask him how he got them. I found out later that he got official permission of the Czech puppet government, with agreement of the Germans, to transfer Czech crowns into pounds, so and so much for each immigrant.

speaker: interviewee
text: When Hitler invaded Czechoslovakia, already when he made us to take [INAUDIBLE], a fund was established-- I think it was $10 million-- to help with immigration. Or 10 million pounds, so it was taken from there, and he had the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the next thing. He sent the money to me. I sent the money to Rand. And the ship was to be there, I think, on the 10th of November. So the group left from Prague, left-- two [? boats ?] left from Prague, for Bratislava, which was, at that time, not part of Czechoslovakia anymore, because it separated Czech from Slovakia, with the capital of Slovakia. The Slovaks were much more [INAUDIBLE] than the Czechs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had a group. We mobilized from Vienna, [? as they come. ?] But I thoroughly didn't-- I didn't expect it to be there on the 20th of November. He was a new man. But I thought it would be there within a week or two of the 20th of November. The boat didn't come.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the people-- there was not enough money, anyhow. These are 2,000 pounds, just the down payment. And we needed money. So there was a group in Hungary, which wanted to leave. So I went to Hungary, via Yugoslavia, again, in order to avoid Germany and Austria, because the same thing at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Hungary, I formed a group with the Agudat Israel, which we tried to avoid, before-- the people who [INAUDIBLE] Agudat Israel, because they are so orthodox, they won't eat anything. They'd rather die than eat. But they gave in, there. And from Agudat Israel and others who wanted to leave. And we soon had enough people, more people than we needed, who wanted to go with the next transport. And I went, I worked this together, with preparations, what has to be done in Budapest with Dr. Kalman. Dr. Kalman was a lawyer, and the president or chairman of the Hungarian revisionist organization. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to clarify one point. You started to work with the orthodox community, because they could pay. Is that-- am I understanding that right? They could pay for the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I didn't have anybody else. When I came here, there was nothing [? had done. ?] We had not enough people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. But I didn't have enough people for the transport. I didn't have enough money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't have enough money, and you didn't have enough people? I'm not sure I'm understanding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I didn't have enough money, and I didn't have enough people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so for both of those reasons. Now I understand. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Agudat was ready to pay. And then the Romania people, whom we had to take, who didn't pay anything-- Betarim, who were young boys, 16 and 18 years old, and who didn't have anything. So we needed the money from the Hungarians. And I waited, now, until the ship comes. And the people from Prague were driven out from Bratislava-- not all of them, but a Betar transport was told, unless you leave in 24 hours or so, we'll hand you over to the Germans, the old story. So they very readily rented, hired, or leased a boat-- not a ship. It was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how do you call the ships, which don't go by their own power. They are pulled by other ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a tug boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a tug-- but it was a real, real, very large one. I don't think there is a motor on that one. And they put everybody on this boat. The name of this boat was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it will come to my mind. This was very hard, to be on this boat, because they were not [INAUDIBLE] towards people. People-- there were no accommodations for people. There were no toilets. There was no way to lie down, and to stand up. And it was winter, by then. But was the name of this barge. and they went-- the went down through [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the sea, to Constanza, to Sulina. Sulina is this town that, then you reach the Red Sea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they waited there. The owners of the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what was the name of the ship? of the pressured, you go, go [INAUDIBLE] still no ships, there. So I found out, now, that Mr. Rand had taken and stolen these 2,000 pounds-- had taken for himself, and tried to get this Turkish ship to get there without any money.

speaker: interviewee
text: He succeeded in getting them there, but now they wanted money. And they wanted the permission to leave-- they are, all the time, they hope that they would get permission from the British, to leave. They were very uninformed about the whole situation. I made a contract. I couldn't get into Turkey, into Romania at that time, because the British had succeeded, in Romania, to put a arrest order on me. So I had to stay in Hungary. And I sent a woman, a Viennese woman, who was a British citizen-- she had married a Viennese guy, who was born in 1914, when the Austrians were [? entered ?] from the Isle of Man, I believe, they were [? entered ?]. And she was born-- he was born in British territory, therefore he was a British citizen. Before she married him, she had a British passport, which enabled her to travel freely. And she was a very clever, very energetic, but highly hysterical woman. Well, we got a treaty with these Turks, with these Turks, that we made, with them, four trips or five trips. And each time, we have more people, the Turkish-- Turk, he didn't realize these Turks, that this is not possible, with one ship-- that the ship will be snapped by the British, obviously. Well, this is what [INAUDIBLE]. I wasn't there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Reuben Hecht was there too. And for me, I sent this woman, Mrs. Bernstein, down there. And there was negotiations done, and I got to the contacts, which I have. I don't have it prepared. I couldn't find it. I had it yesterday, in my hand. But in the meanwhile, I misplaced it again. A contract for this ship. In the meanwhile, people in the were freezing, and we didn't have any money. And some money came from America, but there was still not enough money. And I do not remember that somehow, we had money. I took Mrs. Bernstein into the deal, because she was in charge of the money. And we had some money, and we [? lifted ?] money. We wanted to keep money-- I wanted to have money to buy a ship, on my own, so I'm not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] depending, anymore, on Greeks or Turks. But this was the last ship I made. It's a book out by-- very good book by-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Ofer is her last name. Ofer, Dalia Ofer, of the Hebrew University. On the transport, she has a chapter-- Willy Perl's last ship. It was the largest ship ever to be used in illegal transportation. The ship finally came, but how this was-- it was the ship there, and we didn't have the money to the man. And the Germans, the Austrians had come down, in the meanwhile. And the Germans threatened to send them back to Vienna, because they were justified. It was winter, and the Danube was ready to freeze any moment. It's very cold there, and they needed the ship. It was a war on. They could have been in trouble with the German authorities. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was about the last-- the second time, and the third [INAUDIBLE], they got from the Germans. And now the ship was to leave, with the people, within 24 hours. I was-- at that point, I left Hungary to get to Switzerland, where I can do something from there, because I couldn't do anything from Switzerland, from Hungary. And this was a difficult trip, because I had to go via Yugoslavia, where I had been arrested before, for overstaying.

speaker: interviewee
text: On a transit, we'd enter. I had just stayed. And I did the same thing again. I had a transit visa, and was admitted at the border. And in Belgrade, I left the train. I didn't have the passport. I had left the passport with the passport control, who [INAUDIBLE]. But I left the passport with him. I worry about this later. First of all, I have to see what's going on here. And if I can bring people out from here, from Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause now, to change tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one moment.
